children.name: Salamandra
children.description: Fire salamanders — strikingly patterned, terrestrial salamanders of European forests. The Fire Salamander's vivid yellow-and-black livery is a textbook example of aposematism: the skin glands secrete highly toxic alkaloids (samandarine and congeners) that deter virtually all predators. Aristotle believed it could survive fire — actually it does retreat into wet log piles that end up on fires. It is viviparous, giving birth to fully metamorphosed young or, in alpine populations, to a single fully formed larva. A novel fungal pathogen (*Bsal*) has caused catastrophic declines in the Netherlands.

children.name: Salamandra salamandra
children.description: Europe's most familiar salamander. Nocturnal and secretive, emerging after rain to forage for invertebrates. The parotoid glands behind the eyes produce bufadienolides and samandarine — potent enough to kill small mammals that attempt to eat the animal.

children.name: Salamandra atra
children.description: Uniformly glossy black; adapted to life above 700 m in the Alps and Dinaric mountains. Highly viviparous: gestation lasts 2–4 years, the longest of any salamander. Gives birth to 1–2 fully metamorphosed young — all others are absorbed as nutrition in utero.

children.name: Salamandra infraimmaculata
children.description: A large fire salamander of the Middle East, breeding in temporary rain pools and springs of the eastern Mediterranean. Near Threatened due to water extraction and habitat loss.

children.name: Salamandra algira
children.description: The only fire salamander native to Africa, found in the mountains of Morocco, Algeria and Tunisia, with some populations giving live birth to fully metamorphosed young.

children.name: Salamandra lanzai
children.description: A fully terrestrial, live-bearing salamander restricted to high-altitude meadows of the Cottian Alps on the French–Italian border.

children.name: Salamandra corsica
children.description: A fire salamander endemic to the island of Corsica, formerly considered a subspecies of the European fire salamander.

children.name: Lyciasalamandra
children.description: Lycian salamanders — a genus of viviparous salamanders restricted to the rocky limestone hills and scrub of southern Turkey and adjacent Greek islands. They were formerly treated as a subgenus of Mertensiella before molecular phylogenetics confirmed their distinctness. All species are ovoviviparous, giving birth to fully formed young in late winter. Several species have extremely restricted ranges on single islands or peninsulas, making them acutely vulnerable to fire, grazing and tourist development.

children.name: Lyciasalamandra luschani
children.description: A slender, brown salamander found on rocky limestone outcrops of southwestern Turkey and several Aegean islands, active mainly in autumn and winter. Vulnerable due to its fragmented range and low reproductive rate.

children.name: Lyciasalamandra antalyana
children.description: A limestone-dwelling salamander endemic to the Antalya region of southern Turkey, inhabiting maquis scrub and rocky terrain. Threatened by coastal tourism development and road construction.

children.name: Lyciasalamandra atifi
children.description: A narrowly endemic Lycian salamander of the Bodrum Peninsula, Turkey, with a highly restricted range on rocky maquis slopes. One of several species named for Turkish herpetologists.

children.name: Lyciasalamandra billae
children.description: An endemic Lycian salamander of the Datça Peninsula, Turkey, found in rocky limestone habitats near the Aegean coast. Critically Endangered due to its tiny range.

children.name: Lyciasalamandra fazilae
children.description: A Lycian salamander restricted to Kastellorizo Island and adjacent Turkish coast, one of the most island-confined vertebrates in the western Palaearctic. Its entire global range covers only a few square kilometres.

children.name: Lyciasalamandra helverseni
children.description: A Lycian salamander of the eastern Aegean island of Karpathos and nearby islets, the only member of the genus occurring in Greece. Threatened by drought and overgrazing.

children.name: Lyciasalamandra yehudahi
children.description: A Lycian salamander of the Marmaris–Köyceğiz region of southwestern Turkey, inhabiting garigue and pine woodland on calcareous substrates. Vulnerable to forest fires and overgrazing.

children.name: Salamandrina
children.description: Spectacled salamanders — small, slender salamanders of the Apennine Peninsula, Italy, unique among European salamanders in possessing only four toes on each foot. They have a striking red-orange belly and undersides of the hind limbs that are flashed as a warning display (unken reflex) when threatened. Spectacled salamanders inhabit shaded, humid hill forests, spending winter and summer underground and emerging in spring and autumn to breed in cold, clear streams.

children.name: Salamandrina perspicillata
children.description: A four-toed salamander of the northern and central Apennines, named for the pale spectacle-like marking between the eyes. When threatened it displays its vivid red belly by arching its back — the unken reflex — warning predators of skin toxins.

children.name: Salamandrina terdigitata
children.description: A spectacled salamander of the southern Apennines, described as a distinct species from its northern relative in 2005 based on morphological and molecular differences. It shares the same four-toed feet and aposematic belly display as S. perspicillata.

children.name: Triturus
children.description: Crested newts — Europe's largest and most spectacular newts, named for the dramatic toothed crest that breeding males develop along the back and tail. The Great Crested Newt, with its warty black skin and orange-and-black spotted belly, is one of the most legally protected animals in Britain — a strict planning constraint that has delayed or modified more construction projects than perhaps any other species. Crested newts migrate to ponds in spring, where males perform an elaborate courtship display, pressing the female against the bottom before depositing a spermatophore.

children.name: Triturus cristatus
children.description: Strictly protected under UK and EU law; surveys are required before any development near ponds they may occupy. Declined sharply in Britain due to pond loss and agricultural intensification. Females lay individual eggs wrapped in pond-plant leaves.

children.name: Triturus marmoratus
children.description: Perhaps the most beautiful European newt — bright green marbled with black, with an orange or red vertebral stripe in females. Restricted to Iberia and southwestern France. Hybridises with the Great Crested Newt where ranges overlap.

children.name: Triturus carnifex
children.description: Triturus carnifex (Italian Crested Newt) is a crested newts species found in Europe region.

children.name: Triturus dobrogicus
children.description: A slender crested newt of the lowland Danube floodplain, the most aquatic of the crested newts, breeding in oxbow lakes and marshes.

children.name: Triturus karelinii
children.description: A large crested newt of the Balkans, Anatolia and the Caspian region, the male developing a tall jagged crest in the breeding season.

children.name: Triturus ivanbureschi
children.description: A crested newt of the southern Balkans and western Anatolia, described in 2013 and named after Bulgarian zoologist Ivan Buresch.

children.name: Triturus pygmaeus
children.description: A smaller marbled newt of the southern Iberian Peninsula, distinguished from the marbled newt by size and a dorsal stripe in females.

children.name: Triturus macedonicus
children.description: A crested newt of the Balkans, centred on North Macedonia, Serbia and adjacent countries. Formerly treated as a subspecies of T. carnifex, it was elevated to full species based on allozymic and morphological evidence.

children.name: Triturus rudolfi
children.description: A crested newt of northeastern Turkey and adjacent Georgia near the Black Sea coast, described as a full species in 2013 from populations previously assigned to T. karelinii.

children.name: Triturus anatolicus
children.description: A recently described crested newt of central and northern Anatolia, Turkey, separated from T. karelinii on the basis of genetic and morphological characters. Occurs in forested uplands and breeds in pools and slow streams.

children.name: Lissotriton
children.description: Smooth and palmate newts — the small, smooth-skinned newts of western and central Europe. The Smooth Newt is the most widespread newt in Britain and Europe, found in garden ponds, ditches, and almost any standing water. The Palmate Newt is smaller and favours acidic, shallow ponds on heathland and upland. Breeding males of the Palmate Newt are unmistakable — they develop black webbing between the hind toes and a thread-like tail filament. After the aquatic breeding season, both species disperse onto land and become almost entirely terrestrial.

children.name: Lissotriton vulgaris
children.description: The most common and widespread newt in Britain and central Europe. Breeding males develop a continuous, wavy dorsal crest and ornate spotting. Spends only a few months in water; the rest of the year is spent on land under logs and stones.

children.name: Lissotriton helveticus
children.description: The smallest British newt. The breeding male's webbed hind feet and tail filament are diagnostic. Favours soft, peaty water on heathland and moorland. Less common in gardens than the Smooth Newt.

children.name: Lissotriton boscai
children.description: A small newt endemic to the Iberian Peninsula, common in ponds and slow streams, the male lacking a dorsal crest.

children.name: Lissotriton italicus
children.description: The smallest European newt, endemic to southern Italy, often retaining gilled larval features into adulthood (paedomorphosis).

children.name: Lissotriton montandoni
children.description: A newt of the Carpathian Mountains and adjacent highlands, the breeding male developing a low, smooth dorsal ridge and a filamentous tail tip.

children.name: Lissotriton kosswigi
children.description: A small, slender newt of streams and pools in northwestern Turkey and adjacent Thrace, one of the least-studied European-region newts. Formerly considered a subspecies of L. vulgaris.

children.name: Lissotriton lanthanites
children.description: A smooth newt of the eastern Adriatic coast, Greece and the western Balkans, recognised as a full species from the L. vulgaris complex in recent molecular studies. Inhabits lowland ponds and ditches.

children.name: Lissotriton maltzani
children.description: A smooth newt of the southern Balkans and Peloponnese, Greece, with a spotted throat distinguishing it from co-occurring L. vulgaris. Named after the German naturalist Heinrich von Maltzan.

children.name: Cynops
children.description: Japanese fire-belly newts — a genus of small, aquatic newts endemic to Japan and China. The Japanese Fire Belly Newt is a popular aquarium animal, recognisable by its dark brown to black dorsal surface and vivid red-orange belly — a warning of the potent tetrodotoxin (TTX) produced in the skin. This is the same toxin found in pufferfish; its origin in newts is thought to involve a bacterium, *Pseudoalteromonas*, that manufactures it. Japanese garter snakes that prey on these newts have evolved TTX resistance — a classic co-evolutionary arms race.

children.name: Cynops pyrrhogaster
children.description: Produces tetrodotoxin in the skin — a potent neurotoxin that blocks sodium channels. Predatory snakes (*Thamnophis* spp. introduced to Japan) have co-evolved partial resistance. One of the few salamanders with limb regeneration studied intensively in biomedical research.

children.name: Cynops orientalis
children.description: A small black newt with a bright orange belly, abundant in the international pet trade and native to eastern China, where wild populations are declining.

children.name: Cynops cyanurus
children.description: A fire belly newt of montane ponds in southwestern China, the breeding male showing bluish tints on the tail.

children.name: Cynops ensicauda
children.description: A robust fire belly newt of the southern Ryukyu Islands (Okinawa group) in Japan, the largest member of the genus. Its red-orange belly and flanks serve as warning coloration; the skin contains tetrodotoxin.

children.name: Cynops orphicus
children.description: A small fire belly newt of coastal Fujian Province, China, one of the least studied Cynops species. Known from very few localities and possibly threatened by urbanisation.
children.namedAfter: Orpheus (mythological reference to its isolated, secretive nature)

children.name: Cynops wolterstorffi
children.description: A fire belly newt formerly known from Kunming Lake, Yunnan, China, now almost certainly extinct following drainage and pollution of its sole habitat in the early twentieth century. One of the few confirmed extinct salamanders.

children.name: Cynops chenggongensis
children.description: A recently described fire belly newt from Chenggong County near Kunming, Yunnan, China. Very similar to C. wolterstorffi but persisting in a handful of remnant ponds and critically endangered.

children.name: Cynops fudingensis
children.description: A fire belly newt described in 2012 from Fuding, Fujian, China, on the basis of molecular and morphological differences from C. orphicus. Inhabits montane streams and hillside pools.

children.name: Pleurodeles
children.description: Ribbed newts — remarkable salamanders with a unique defensive mechanism: when seized by a predator, they flex their ribs outward, puncturing the sides of their body with the rib tips, which then protrude through toxic skin glands, coating the ribs in poison and presenting them to the predator. Remarkably, the skin heals within days. The Spanish Ribbed Newt has been carried to space aboard the Soviet Bion satellites and the International Space Station for microgravity research on amphibian development.

children.name: Pleurodeles waltl
children.description: Europe's largest newt. Uses ribs as delivery needles for skin toxins when attacked. Has flown on four space missions, making it one of the most-used animals in space biology. A model organism for regeneration research.

children.name: Pleurodeles nebulosus
children.description: A ribbed newt of northeastern Algeria and Tunisia, using the same rib-piercing defence as its Iberian relative. Endangered by wetland loss.

children.name: Pleurodeles poireti
children.description: A range-restricted ribbed newt of the Edough Peninsula in Algeria, threatened by habitat degradation.

children.name: Notophthalmus
children.description: Eastern North American newts, including the eastern newt with its terrestrial 'red eft' juvenile stage. Their skin produces potent tetrodotoxin.

children.name: Notophthalmus viridescens
children.description: A widespread eastern North American newt with a striking terrestrial juvenile 'red eft' stage and a toxic skin. A classic model in regeneration and developmental biology research.

children.name: Notophthalmus perstriatus
children.description: A small newt of the southeastern United States associated with ephemeral ponds in pine flatwoods, declining and a candidate for federal protection.

children.name: Notophthalmus meridionalis
children.description: A boldly spotted newt of coastal south Texas and northeastern Mexico, inhabiting shallow ponds in subtropical thorn scrub. Threatened by habitat loss and drought in its limited range.

children.name: Taricha
children.description: Pacific newts of western North America, famous for the extreme tetrodotoxin toxicity of some species, the focus of a classic predator–prey coevolutionary arms race with garter snakes.

children.name: Taricha granulosa
children.description: One of the most toxic amphibians, its skin laden with tetrodotoxin; it is the central player in a famous coevolutionary arms race with resistant garter snakes along the Pacific coast.

children.name: Taricha torosa
children.description: A toxic newt of coastal California known for large breeding migrations to streams and ponds, increasingly threatened by drought and introduced predators.

children.name: Taricha rivularis
children.description: A stream-breeding newt of coastal northern California with a vivid red belly, showing strong homing fidelity to its natal breeding stream.

children.name: Taricha sierrae
children.description: A Pacific newt of the Sierra Nevada foothills and adjacent central California ranges, distinguished from T. torosa by a darker eye iris. Breeds in foothill streams and ponds of the Central Valley watershed.

children.name: Tylototriton
children.description: Asian crocodile newts, robust knobby-skinned salamanders of montane Asia with prominent bony head ridges and warty glands. Many species are threatened by the pet trade.

children.name: Tylototriton verrucosus
children.description: A robust knobby crocodile newt of montane Asia from the Himalayas to Southeast Asia, heavily collected for the pet and traditional medicine trades.

children.name: Tylototriton shanjing
children.description: A strikingly orange-and-black crocodile newt of Yunnan, China, popular in the pet trade and threatened by overcollection and habitat loss.

children.name: Tylototriton kweichowensis
children.description: A crocodile newt of Guizhou and Yunnan in China with reddish dorsal ridges and tail, threatened by collection.

children.name: Tylototriton asperrimus
children.description: A uniformly dark, heavily tuberculated crocodile newt of Guangxi and adjacent provinces of China, breeding in montane forest pools. Threatened by habitat loss and collection for the pet and medicinal markets.

children.name: Tylototriton himalayanus
children.description: A crocodile newt of the eastern Himalayas in Sikkim and West Bengal, India, with a dark body and orange dorsal ridge and tail. One of the higher-altitude members of the genus.

children.name: Tylototriton taliangensis
children.description: A crocodile newt of the Daliang Mountains of Sichuan, China, recognised by its relatively slender build and pale dorsal ridges. Found in cool montane forests up to 2500 m.

children.name: Tylototriton wenxianensis
children.description: A crocodile newt from Wenxian County in Gansu Province, China, first described in 1979. It inhabits rice paddies and forest pools and has been heavily exploited for the traditional medicine trade.

children.name: Tylototriton yangi
children.description: A crocodile newt of Yunnan, described in 2012, with prominent orange parotoid glands and a robust body. Named for Chinese herpetologist Datong Yang.

children.name: Tylototriton notialis
children.description: A crocodile newt described from the highlands of Thailand and Myanmar, one of the more southerly members of the genus. Inhabits montane forests and breeds in still hillside pools.

children.name: Tylototriton panhai
children.description: A Thai crocodile newt described in 2013, named for Thai biologist Sunchai Panha. Found in highland forest in northern Thailand, with a limited range and threatened by collection.

children.name: Tylototriton ziegleri
children.description: A crocodile newt of northern Vietnam described in 2014, named for herpetologist Thomas Ziegler. Inhabits subtropical montane forest and is threatened by deforestation and collection.

children.name: Tylototriton anguliceps
children.description: A crocodile newt described from Laos and adjacent Vietnam, characterised by strongly angular head ridges and a robust orange-marked body. Threatened by habitat loss in the Annamite Mountains.

children.name: Tylototriton vietnamensis
children.description: A crocodile newt endemic to northern Vietnam, known from montane forest at elevations above 1000 m. Heavily exploited for the international pet trade and listed as Endangered.

children.name: Tylototriton pseudoverrucosus
children.description: A crocodile newt of Yunnan and adjacent Myanmar, closely resembling T. verrucosus and long confused with it. Separated on molecular and morphological grounds in 2013.

children.name: Tylototriton pulcherrimus
children.description: A crocodile newt of Yunnan and Guangxi, China, noted for its particularly vivid orange-and-black colouring. The species name means 'most beautiful', reflecting its striking appearance.

children.name: Tylototriton shanorum
children.description: A crocodile newt of the Shan Hills of Myanmar, described in 2013. Lives in montane forest pools at moderate elevations and is threatened by agricultural encroachment.

children.name: Tylototriton daweishanensis
children.description: A crocodile newt described in 2012 from the Dawei Mountain area of Yunnan, distinguished from related species by head shape and colouration. Threatened by forest degradation.

children.name: Tylototriton lizhenchangi
children.description: A recently described crocodile newt from the highlands of Myanmar, named for Chinese herpetologist Li Zhenchang. Inhabits montane forests and forest-edge pools.

children.name: Tylototriton neoparvus
children.description: A small-bodied crocodile newt from Thailand, described in 2014 on the basis of morphological and molecular characters. Inhabits highland forest in northern and western Thailand.

children.name: Tylototriton liuyangensis
children.description: A crocodile newt described from Liuyang, Hunan Province, China, in 2012. Inhabits montane forests and breeds in ponds and slow streams at mid-elevation.

children.name: Tylototriton uyenoi
children.description: A crocodile newt of Thailand and Myanmar, named for Japanese ichthyologist Teruya Uyeno. Found in subtropical and montane forest habitats in the northern portion of its range.

children.name: Tylototriton wurmli
children.description: A crocodile newt from the highlands of northern Myanmar, described relatively recently. Named for collector M. Wurml. Inhabits dense montane forest at higher elevations.

children.name: Tylototriton broadoridgus
children.description: A crocodile newt of Hunan Province, China, characterised by its proportionally broad dorsal ridge. Described in 2012 and considered Vulnerable due to collection pressure and habitat loss.

children.name: Tylototriton dabienicus
children.description: A crocodile newt from the Dabie Mountains along the Hubei–Henan border in China, described in 2012. One of the more northerly members of the genus, inhabiting broadleaf forest pools.

children.name: Tylototriton yangshengi
children.description: A crocodile newt from Fujian and Guangdong, China, described in 2014. Named for contributions to Chinese herpetology. Inhabits subtropical hill forests and breeds in still ponds.

children.name: Tylototriton kachinorum
children.description: A crocodile newt described from Kachin State in northern Myanmar, one of the most northerly Myanmar species of the genus. Inhabits dense, humid montane forest.

children.name: Tylototriton rogerbouri
children.description: A crocodile newt of Vietnam described in 2014 and named in honour of French herpetologist Roger Bour. Found in subtropical montane forest in the northern highlands.

children.name: Tylototriton pasmansi
children.description: A crocodile newt described from Vietnam in 2016, named after Belgian herpetologist Frank Pasmans who has studied Bsal disease in salamanders. Inhabits highland subtropical forest.

children.name: Echinotriton
children.description: Asian spiny newts whose sharp rib tips can pierce the skin as a defence, found on islands and montane regions of East Asia.

children.name: Echinotriton andersoni
children.description: A spiny newt of the Ryukyu Islands of Japan and parts of China, endangered by habitat loss, whose sharp ribs can pierce its own skin in defence.

children.name: Echinotriton chinhaiensis
children.description: A critically endangered spiny newt known from only a handful of sites in Zhejiang Province, China. First described in 1930 and considered one of the rarest salamanders on Earth, it has been collected intensively for the pet trade.

children.name: Echinotriton maxiquadratus
children.description: A spiny newt described from Hainan Island, China, in 2013, characterised by a proportionally large quadrate bone. Inhabits tropical montane forest on the island.

children.name: Echinotriton slowinski
children.description: A spiny newt of Myanmar described in 2012 and named in memory of herpetologist Joseph Slowinski, who died in Myanmar in 2001. Found in subtropical hill forest.

children.name: Neurergus
children.description: Mountain newts of the Middle East, brightly patterned stream-dwellers of the Zagros and Taurus mountains, several critically endangered and prized in the illegal pet trade.

children.name: Neurergus kaiseri
children.description: A boldly black, white and orange newt endemic to a few streams in the Zagros Mountains of Iran, once critically endangered by the international pet trade and now protected under CITES.

children.name: Neurergus crocatus
children.description: A mountain newt of streams in Iran, Iraq and Turkey, vulnerable to habitat loss and collection.

children.name: Neurergus strauchii
children.description: A yellow-spotted mountain newt restricted to highland streams of eastern Turkey, with a small fragmented range.

children.name: Neurergus microspilotus
children.description: A critically endangered mountain newt known from only a small number of streams in the Kermanshah and Kurdistan provinces of western Iran. Threatened by water extraction for agriculture and intense pet trade collection.

children.name: Neurergus derjugini
children.description: A mountain newt of streams in the Zagros foothills of Iran and Iraq, with white or yellow spots on a dark background. Less severely threatened than N. kaiseri but still impacted by drought and habitat modification.

children.name: Paramesotriton
children.description: Asian warty newts of streams in southern China and Vietnam, with rough granular skin and a largely aquatic lifestyle.

children.name: Paramesotriton hongkongensis
children.description: A largely aquatic warty newt of cool, clear streams in Hong Kong and adjacent Guangdong, protected under Hong Kong law.

children.name: Paramesotriton chinensis
children.description: A rough-skinned aquatic newt of streams in eastern China, commonly seen in the pet trade.

children.name: Paramesotriton caudopunctatus
children.description: A warty newt of cool streams in Guizhou and adjacent Hunan, China, distinguishable by spots on the undersurface of the tail. Inhabits fast-flowing forest streams with rocky substrate.

children.name: Paramesotriton deloustali
children.description: A warty newt of northern Vietnam and adjacent Yunnan, China, the most southerly member of the genus. Described in 1934 and found in montane forest streams at moderate to high elevations.

children.name: Paramesotriton fuzhongensis
children.description: A warty newt described from Fuzhong County, Yunnan Province, China. Inhabits subtropical and montane stream margins; threatened by collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Paramesotriton guanxiensis
children.description: A warty newt from Guangxi, China, inhabiting montane forest streams. Described in 1979 and notable for its strongly keeled tail used in swimming through rocky stream habitat.

children.name: Paramesotriton labiatus
children.description: A warty newt of Yunnan and Guangxi in China, characterised by distinct labial folds that give the mouth a lipped appearance. Found in cool montane streams.

children.name: Paramesotriton laoensis
children.description: A warty newt from highland streams in northern Laos and adjacent China, described in 2004. Inhabits cool, clear streams in subtropical mountain forest.

children.name: Paramesotriton maolanensis
children.description: A warty newt of the Maolan Karst Forest Nature Reserve, Guizhou, China, described in 2008. Its range largely coincides with a UNESCO World Heritage Site, affording some protection.

children.name: Paramesotriton qingyuanensis
children.description: A warty newt from Qingyuan County, Zhejiang Province, China, described in 2008. Inhabits forested hill streams and is threatened by urbanisation and collection.

children.name: Paramesotriton yunwuensis
children.description: A warty newt from the Yunwu Mountains of Guizhou, China, described in 2012. Found in clear mountain streams under rocks and submerged vegetation.

children.name: Paramesotriton zhijinensis
children.description: A warty newt from Zhijin County, Guizhou, China, described in 2008. Among the most recently described members of the genus, living in cool, fast-flowing karst streams.

children.name: Paramesotriton longliensis
children.description: A warty newt from Longli County, Guizhou Province, China, described in 2004. Inhabits mountain streams in subtropical broad-leaved forest and breeds in spring.

children.name: Paramesotriton wulingensis
children.description: A warty newt from the Wuling Mountains of Hunan and Guizhou, China, described in 2012. Known from cool, shaded hill streams in intact broadleaf forest.

children.name: Paramesotriton ermizhaoi
children.description: A warty newt from Guangdong Province, China, named for Chinese herpetologist Ermi Zhao. Inhabits subtropical hill streams and forest ponds.

children.name: Euproctus
children.description: European brook salamanders endemic to the mountain streams of Corsica and Sardinia, adapted to cold, fast-flowing water.

children.name: Euproctus platycephalus
children.description: An endangered brook salamander endemic to the cold mountain streams of Sardinia, threatened by water abstraction, introduced trout and disease.

children.name: Euproctus montanus
children.description: A brook salamander endemic to the mountain streams and forests of Corsica, with reduced lungs as an adaptation to fast-flowing water.

children.name: Calotriton
children.description: Pyrenean brook newts, cold-water specialists of mountain streams in the Pyrenees and nearby ranges.

children.name: Calotriton asper
children.description: A cold-water newt of Pyrenean mountain streams and lakes, with rough skin and reduced lungs, some populations remaining gilled and aquatic as adults.

children.name: Calotriton arnoldi
children.description: A critically endangered newt restricted to a handful of streams in the Montseny massif of Catalonia, one of Europe's most threatened amphibians.

children.name: Chioglossa
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the golden-striped salamander, a slender, long-tailed salamander endemic to northwest Iberia.

children.name: Chioglossa lusitanica
children.description: A slender, long-tailed salamander of humid forests in northwest Iberia, with two golden dorsal stripes and the ability to shed its tail to escape predators.

children.name: Mertensiella
children.description: The Caucasian salamander, a slender stream-associated salamander of the Caucasus region.

children.name: Mertensiella caucasica
children.description: A slender, long-tailed salamander of mountain streams in the Caucasus of Turkey and Georgia, Vulnerable and threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Ichthyosaura
children.description: The alpine newt, a widespread European newt of montane and lowland pools, the males brilliantly coloured in the breeding season.

children.name: Ichthyosaura alpestris
children.description: A widespread European newt of montane and lowland pools, the breeding male brilliantly coloured with a blue body and orange belly. Some high-altitude populations remain permanently aquatic and gilled.

children.name: Ommatotriton
children.description: Banded newts of the eastern Mediterranean and Middle East, the breeding males developing a high, banded crest.

children.name: Ommatotriton vittatus
children.description: A banded newt of the eastern Mediterranean and Middle East, the breeding male developing a tall, smooth-edged crest and a silvery flank stripe.

children.name: Ommatotriton ophryticus
children.description: A banded newt of the eastern Black Sea coast, Turkey, Georgia and adjacent areas, breeding in small pools and slow streams in woodland. The breeding male bears a high, spotted crest.

children.name: Pachytriton
children.description: Chinese paddle-tail newts, smooth-skinned aquatic newts of cool mountain streams in southern China.

children.name: Pachytriton labiatus
children.description: A smooth-skinned, fully aquatic paddle-tail newt of cool mountain streams in southern China, popular in the aquarium trade.

children.name: Pachytriton brevipes
children.description: A paddle-tail newt of montane streams in Fujian, Zhejiang and Jiangxi, China, distinguished by dark spots on a brownish body. One of the more commonly traded species in the aquarium hobby.

children.name: Pachytriton feii
children.description: A paddle-tail newt of Guangdong and Fujian, China, described in 2012 and named for herpetologist Liang Fei. Inhabits cool, fast-flowing hill streams in primary subtropical forest.

children.name: Pachytriton granulosus
children.description: A paddle-tail newt from the Wuyishan region of Fujian and Jiangxi, China, with slightly rough (granular) skin relative to other congeners. Described in 2012.

children.name: Pachytriton inexpectatus
children.description: A paddle-tail newt discovered in Guangdong Province, China, described as an unexpected new species in 2012 from a region previously thought to lack Pachytriton.

children.name: Pachytriton maborensis
children.description: A paddle-tail newt from Mabor, Jiangxi Province, China, one of several species described during a taxonomic revision of the genus in 2012. Found in cool montane forest streams.

children.name: Pachytriton archospotus
children.description: A paddle-tail newt of Hunan and Guangxi, China, named for the prominent spots near the base of the tail. Described in 2008 following multi-year field surveys in the Nanling Mountains.

children.name: Pachytriton xanthospilos
children.description: A paddle-tail newt of Zhejiang and Anhui, China, distinguishable by bright yellow or orange spots on a dark brown body. Described in 2012 as part of the comprehensive Pachytriton revision.

children.name: Laotriton
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Laotian newt, a large warty newt of high-elevation pools in Laos, heavily exploited for the pet trade.

children.name: Laotriton laoensis
children.description: A large warty newt of high-elevation pools in northern Laos, Endangered and heavily exploited for the international pet trade and local consumption.
